one_line_takeaway: P2F trains one text-conditioned parameter generator per base LLM that then produces LoRA fingerprint deltas for arbitrary new trigger/target descriptions in a single forward pass, achieving >96% fingerprint-injection success across 15 open-source model configurations with only minor capability side-effects and reasonable robustness to quantization and fine-tuning -- but the paper reports no comparison against any prior fingerprinting baseline.
key_finding: At default 32-bit precision, FSR (BLEU/ROUGE-1/ROUGE-L) exceeds 96% for every one of the 15 model configurations, reaching 100% for some (e.g., Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct FSR_BLEU=100.0%). Under quantization robustness testing, FSR remains high after compression: at 8-bit, FSR_BLEU values range from 94.3% (Llama3.2-3B-Instruct) up to 99.7-100% for several Qwen configurations, i.e. degradation from 32-bit baseline is generally only a few points. Harmlessness: average score change across the three benchmarks (MultiRC/LogiQA/OpenBookQA) after fingerprint injection is mostly positive or mildly negative, ranging from -2.48 (Mistral-7B-Base) to +6.44 (Llama3.2-1B-Instruct); Llama-7B and Mistral-7B show a net decline while most other models show slight net improvement (the authors attribute gains partly to the regularization samples used during training, which they suggest boost instruction-following). Downstream fine-tuning on Alpaca/ChatDoctor: fingerprints are reported as retained with 'nearly perfect resilience' across most models (detailed per-model numbers deferred to an appendix not fully captured here). Ablations on Qwen2.5-0.5B: removing the Residual Prediction (RP) component drops FSR_BLEU from 99.0% (default) to 92.7%; removing the Layer-wise Scale (LS) mechanism drops it more mildly to 98.3% -- confirming RP is the more load-bearing stability component. LoRA-rank ablation (rank 2/4/8/16) shows FSR staying in a narrow 97.7-99.0% band, indicating the generator is not sensitive to a specific low-rank capacity. Critically, the paper explicitly declines to report any baseline comparison against prior fingerprinting methods (WLM, IF, UTF, MYL, FP-VEC, EditMark, PlugAE, iSeal, etc.), arguing the zero-shot-generalization-to-unseen-fingerprints setting is not comparable to methods that fine-tune to memorize one fixed trigger-response pair -- so all effectiveness numbers above are self-reported with no external baseline.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is Prompt2Fingerprint (P2F), the first framework that reformulates LLM fingerprinting (a proactive/active ownership-verification technique) as a conditional parameter-generation task rather than a per-fingerprint fine-tuning task. A single trained 'parameter generator' (a Transformer with a token-level-embedding-conditioned feature extractor and a non-autoregressive parameter decoder) maps an arbitrary textual fingerprint description (trigger + target response) directly to LoRA weight increments (matrices A, B) in one forward pass. These deltas are injected into a frozen base LLM via forward hooks (y = Wx + (alpha/r)*BAx) to implant trigger-response behavior, without any additional per-fingerprint model fine-tuning. Three concrete architectural contributions are claimed: (1) reformulating fingerprint injection as conditional parameter generation (RQ1); (2) a token-level embedding conditioning mechanism (retaining full token-level text embeddings instead of pooled sentence embeddings) so the generator captures fine-grained, instruction-level trigger/target semantics rather than only coarse task-level signals (RQ2); (3) an end-to-end training scheme using the LLM's own SFT cross-entropy loss (trained via forward hooks, generator backbone optimized while LLM and embedding model are frozen) combined with a 'stable initialization and residual prediction' strategy (A = fixed Gaussian basis + learnable residual; B = learnable scalar g times a predicted residual, g initialized to 0) and a per-layer learnable scale coefficient, which together avoid the prior two-stage paradigm's need to pretrain/store large corpora of description-checkpoint parameter pairs and avoid the resulting error propagation (RQ3).
threat_model: This is a defensive IP-protection / ownership-verification mechanism, not an attack paper. The provider is the defender: they train one parameter generator per base LLM, then instantly generate and inject a unique fingerprint (trigger + target output, e.g. embedding a user ID or license identifier) for each of potentially thousands of downstream redistributed model copies without retraining. Verification is black-box: the provider queries a suspect deployed model with the secret trigger and checks whether it returns the predetermined target response. The adversary is a party who has obtained a fingerprinted model copy and wants to remove or evade the fingerprint (to redistribute the model without attribution) while otherwise preserving model utility. The paper explicitly tests two removal/evasion vectors: (1) quantization (32-bit to 16-bit/8-bit) as a fingerprint-erasure technique, and (2) downstream fine-tuning (on Alpaca and ChatDoctor datasets) intended to overwrite the injected trigger-response behavior via gradient updates. The paper does not model adaptive/white-box attackers who know the P2F generator architecture, nor forgery/false-claim attacks, nor attempts to detect or filter fingerprint triggers from input traffic.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitation (Limitations section) is narrow: it remains open how well a trained generator for one base architecture generalizes across different model architectures/configurations, and they suggest future work could feed architectural metadata (layer configuration, hidden dimensions) as additional conditioning input. Beyond the authors' own text: the paper explicitly has no baseline comparison against any prior fingerprinting method, by its own admission, so absolute claims of practicality/superiority over prior proactive fingerprinting cannot be verified from this paper alone. The generator must still be trained per base LLM (25-120 GPU-hours), so the scalability gain applies specifically to the many-fingerprints-per-base-model regime, not to a zero-training-cost setting. The threat model tested covers only quantization and benign downstream fine-tuning as removal attacks; no adaptive attacker aware of the P2F mechanism, no fingerprint-forgery/false-claim scenario, and no evaluation against dedicated fingerprint-detection or fingerprint-scrubbing techniques from the fingerprinting-robustness literature (e.g., targeted pruning, model merging, adversarial trigger search) is reported in the visible text.

datasets_used: self-constructed fingerprint description dataset, N=300 held-out test descriptions for FSR evaluation
quantitative_results: FSR (BLEU/ROUGE-1/ROUGE-L) at 32-bit >96% across all 15 model configs, up to 100% for some (e.g. Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct FSR_BLEU=100.0%)
FSR at 8-bit quantization ranges from 94.3% (Llama3.2-3B-Instruct, FSR_BLEU) to up to 100% for some Qwen configs
Harmlessness average delta across MultiRC/LogiQA/OpenBookQA ranges from -2.48 (Mistral-7B-Base) to +6.44 (Llama3.2-1B-Instruct)
Ablation: removing Residual Prediction drops FSR_BLEU on Qwen2.5-0.5B from 99.0% to 92.7%
Ablation: removing Layer-wise Scale drops FSR_BLEU on Qwen2.5-0.5B from 99.0% to 98.3%
LoRA-rank ablation (2/4/8/16) on Qwen2.5-0.5B keeps FSR_BLEU within 98.3%-99.0%
Training cost per base LLM: ~25-120 GPU-hours on a single RTX A6000 (48GB)
baselines_compared: None -- the paper explicitly states it omits comparison to prior LLM fingerprinting methods (e.g. WLM, Instructional Fingerprinting/IF, UTF, MYL, FP-VEC, EditMark, PlugAE, iSeal), arguing the zero-shot generalization-to-unseen-descriptions setting is not a fair comparison against methods that fine-tune to memorize a single fixed trigger-response pair
